title: A Unified microscopic picture of cation and anion migration in MAPbI$_3$
authors: Viren Tyagi, Geert Brocks, Shuxia Tao
abstract: Passivating defects and restricting defect mobilities in halide perovskites to increase device lifetimes has become a main field of research. Modeling structure and mobility of point defects is an essential contribution to this endeavor. We employ molecular dynamics, based on neural network potentials trained on density functional theory data, to model ion migration in MAPbI$_3$ triggered by I and MA vacancies or interstitials. Most of these species diffuse rapidly at room temperature, with migration barriers between 0.15 and 0.20 eV. MA interstitials are highly mobile despite their molecular nature, owing to a concerted migration mechanism involving multiple MA ions. No evidence of MA vacancy migration is obtained. Whereas diffusion of I-related defects appreciably depends on their charge state, diffusion of MA defects does not. These results revise the conventional picture of ion transport in hybrid perovskites and highlight the role of collective molecular motion in enabling fast ionic migration.
summary: This paper uses machine-learning-based molecular dynamics to show that methylammonium cations in perovskites can migrate as fast as iodide ions through a collective motion mechanism. This finding revises the traditional understanding of ion transport in solar cell materials and helps reconcile conflicting experimental data.
main_problem: The study aims to resolve discrepancies in reported migration barriers for iodide and methylammonium defects in MAPbI3 and to understand the microscopic mechanisms of cation and anion migration.
main_result: The researchers discovered that MA interstitials migrate as rapidly as iodide defects due to a concerted motion of three neighboring MA ions, and they found that MA vacancy migration is negligible, providing a unified picture of ion transport.
method: Molecular dynamics simulations were performed using neural network potentials (Allegro model) trained on DFT data (SCAN+rVV10 functional) and validated using the climbing image nudged elastic band method.
model_or_system: The physical system is the hybrid organic-inorganic perovskite MAPbI3, specifically focusing on the movement of iodide (anion) and methylammonium (cation) point defects including vacancies and interstitials.
key_observables: Migration barriers (Eb), diffusion coefficients (D), activation energies (Ea), and pre-exponential factors (D0).
important_parameters: Temperature range of 500 K to 600 K for MD simulations; migration barriers between 0.15 and 0.20 eV.
main_assumptions: The neural network potential is assumed to accurately reproduce DFT-level accuracy for forces and energies; the study focuses on intrinsic bulk diffusion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares energy profiles of migration paths between DFT and NNP; Figure 2 shows temperature-dependent diffusion coefficients via Arrhenius plots; Figure 3 provides a schematic of the migration mechanism.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of defect mobility, describes the development and validation of the neural network potential, presents the results of MD simulations for various defect species, and concludes by comparing findings to experimental literature.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Energies along the NEB migration paths, calculated using DFT and the NNPs. The points are the calculated values, and the lines guide the eye; the energy of the minima is set to 0.

Figure 2: Temperature-dependent diffusion coef- ficients of iodide interstitial (I− I ), iodide vacancy (V+ I ), neutral MA interstitial (I0 MA), and positive MA interstitial (I+ MA) defects in MAPbI3. The dashed lines represent the fits to an Arrhenius ex- pression, and the error bars represent the standard error in mean at each point.
Figure 3: Schematic representation of the diffusion of the MA interstitial. The red arrows represent the rotation of the MA ion, and the green arrows represent the migration paths.
